video_title: China's Central Bank Hints at New Policy Framework | The China Show 6/17/2026
gist: Bloomberg's China Show covers a regime where the inflation/war trade is unwinding: a US-Iran MOU reopens the Strait of Hormuz toll-free with oil near $80, Kevin Warsh's first FOMC is expected to shift the Fed toward neutral/hawkish with deliberately less communication, the PBOC signals a move toward an overnight reverse repo policy rate and RMB internationalization, and a fresh China AI sell-off plays out against DeepSeek's price war and Anthropic's export-control retreat that is becoming free advertising for Chinese open-source models.

themes.theme: Warsh Fed and the rate-path debate

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh's first FOMC is expected to shift the Fed statement from slightly neutral/dovish to neutral/hawkish, with the market focus on the change in stance and the size of internal committee divisions rather than the policy action itself, which is expected to be no change.
insights.speaker: Carlos Casanova, senior Asia economist (UBP)
insights.so_what: A hawkish tonal shift with no action keeps a hike priced; watch the statement language for confirmation and position for dollar/yield volatility on division.
insights.quote: WE DO THINK THAT WILL BE A SHIFT IN SENTIMENT FROM A STATEMENT BEING SLIGHTLY NEUTRAL/DOVISH TO BEING NEUTRAL/HAWKISH.

insights.claim: Removal of the word 'additional' from the FOMC statement's reference to the extent and timing of policy adjustments would be read as a hawkish tilt opening the door to a future hike, and three regional presidents had dissented on removing that word in the last three meetings.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A single-word change is the cleanest tradable signal of the Fed's next-move bias for dollar and rates desks.
insights.quote: THE WORD OF THE DAY TODAY IS ADDITIONAL.

insights.claim: Warsh dislikes forward guidance and quantitative easing and prefers rate cuts over buying risk assets, so his first meeting may begin shifting the Fed away from Bernanke-style transparency toward a Greenspan-like model of deliberate ambiguity, possibly without submitting his own dot in the dot plot.
insights.speaker: guest, economist
insights.so_what: Less guidance raises the volatility premium on FOMC days and reduces the dot plot's value as a signaling tool; QT/tapering becomes the thing to watch.
insights.quote: KEEP IN MIND THAT HE DOESN'T LIKE QUANTITATIVE EASING. TAPERING IS ANOTHER THING TO WATCH OUT FOR. HE PREFERS RATE CUTS OVER BUYING RISK ASSETS.

insights.claim: UBP holds a non-consensus view that the Fed will hold rather than hike and could open a window to cut from the second quarter of next year, arguing the central bank is behind the curve with flat wages, a stable 4.3% unemployment rate, and core inflation anchored near a 3% target.
insights.speaker: Carlos Casanova, senior Asia economist (UBP)
insights.so_what: If the market migrates toward this dovish view, expect dollar weakness; it is a contrarian setup against priced-in hikes.
insights.quote: OUR VIEW IS DEFINITELY NOT CONSENSUS RIGHT NOW. THE RISK HERE'S AT THE MARKET BEGINS TO MOVE TOWARD OUR VIEW AND THAT COULD MEAN SOME WEAKNESS ON THE DOLLAR FRONT.

insights.claim: StashAway expects the Fed to hold rather than hike, noting the Fed has not hiked into a second half containing a midterm election in the past 50 years and can now frame the Hormuz-driven oil spike as transient now that the strait has reopened.
insights.speaker: guest, CIO of StashAway
insights.so_what: A 'no hike' outcome against a priced summer hike is a potential risk-on catalyst for equities and a dollar headwind.
insights.quote: THE FED HAS NOT HIKED INTO A SECOND HALF WHERE THERE IS A MIDTERM ELECTION IN THE PAST 50 YEARS.

insights.claim: Bloomberg's US 10-year Treasury yield is around 4.4%-4.5%, and UBP expects yields to stay within a 4%-4.5% range with a move higher requiring more to change.
insights.speaker: Carlos Casanova, senior Asia economist (UBP)
insights.so_what: A defined 4-4.5% range frames duration risk/reward around the Warsh-era Fed.

themes.theme: US-Iran deal and the Hormuz reopening

insights.claim: A 14-point US-Iran MOU draft obtained by Bloomberg would have the US immediately lift some sanctions allowing Iran to resume selling oil, alongside a vaguely defined $300 billion development fund whose payment mechanisms are unclear and possibly funded by Gulf states.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg News managing editor
insights.so_what: Immediate sanctions relief on Iranian oil is the key oil-bearish component traders are pricing; the fund's vagueness is execution risk.
insights.quote: THE U.S. WOULD LOOK TO IMMEDIATELY LIFT SOME SANCTIONS THAT WOULD ALLOW IRAN TO ... START SELLING OIL.

insights.claim: Lifting or waiving sanctions on Iran's oil sales plus facilitating banking and insurance services is likely to generate between $5 billion and $10 billion of Iranian export revenue based on pre-war export volumes, with the MOU signing expected in Switzerland on Friday.
insights.speaker: Chris Kennedy, Bloomberg Economics statecraft lead
insights.so_what: Quantifies the supply/revenue impact of the deal; the Friday signing is the binary event for the oil and tanker trade.
insights.quote: THAT ALONE IS LIKELY TO GENERATE BETWEEN $5 BILLION AND $10 BILLION FOR IRAN'S EXPORT REVENUE BASED ON PRE-WAR EXPORT VOLUMES.

insights.claim: Two Iranian vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz into the Gulf of Oman and Brent fell roughly 4% to below $80, but tanker traffic remains nowhere near pre-war levels, making the durability of flows the next thing to watch.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Oil's move below $80 unwinds the war/inflation premium; a stall in tanker recovery would reverse the bearish oil narrative.

insights.claim: President Trump said the Strait of Hormuz will reopen toll-free and that the deal gives him 99.9% of what he wanted because Iran will never have a nuclear weapon, though he has wanted total US control over the strait.
insights.speaker: Donald Trump
insights.so_what: Toll-free reopening removes a tail risk for energy logistics and shipping insurance pricing.
insights.quote: I WOULD SAY THAT IS 99.9% OF WHAT I WANTED ... IN ADDITION TO THAT THE STRAIT WILL BE OPEN TOLL-FREE.

insights.claim: About 90% of everything that flows through the Strait of Hormuz ends up in Asia, so Asian central banks are reacting more aggressively to the energy disruption than the US, which is comparatively insulated.
insights.speaker: Carlos Casanova, senior Asia economist (UBP)
insights.so_what: Explains the divergence in monetary policy: Asian hikes versus a Fed that can look through transitory oil inflation.
insights.quote: ABOUT 90% OF EVERYTHING THAT FLOWS THROUGH THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ ... UP IN ASIA.

insights.claim: UBP expects oil to settle around $80 per barrel rather than reverting to $60 even with a peace deal, and flags fertilizer supply-chain disruptions and high Chinese food prices as the bigger second-round inflation risk for Asia, potentially forcing rate hikes in Australia and India through 2026.
insights.speaker: Carlos Casanova, senior Asia economist (UBP)
insights.so_what: Watch fertilizer and food inflation as the channel that keeps Asian central banks tightening through 2026 even as oil eases.
insights.quote: THE MAIN THING THAT WORRIES ME IS FOOD PRICE INFLATION. WE ARE SEEING DISRUPTIONS TO FERTILIZER SUPPLY CHAINS.

themes.theme: PBOC policy-framework shift and RMB internationalization

insights.claim: The PBOC governor signaled at the Lujiazui Forum that China will increase overnight reverse repo operations, which markets read as a move toward making the overnight reverse repo rate the policy rate in place of the current seven-day reverse repo rate.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A shift to an overnight policy rate gives the PBOC tighter control of the short end and more flexibility to signal inflation dynamics.
insights.quote: PBOC TO IMPROVE OVERNIGHT REVERSE REPO MECHANISM.

insights.claim: Standard Chartered argued as early as last week that an overnight rate would replace the current seven-day reverse repo rate, part of a multiyear PBOC reform that moved the benchmark from the MLF to the seven-day repo since May 2025 and now toward closer alignment with the Fed and ECB.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Convergence toward DM-style short-rate frameworks improves policy transmission and the readability of Chinese monetary signals.

insights.claim: The PBOC framed giving overseas institutions access to RMB liquidity as building on last year's RMB internationalization theme, encouraging foreign institutions to use and hold renminbi for trade financing and longer-term rather than just transactional purposes, and is exploring an RMB FX futures trade.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low, Bloomberg China correspondent
insights.so_what: Deeper offshore RMB liquidity and FX futures support the long-RMB internationalization thesis and broaden hedging access for foreigners.
insights.quote: THIS PROBABLY GIVES MORE REASON FOR OVERSEAS INSTITUTIONS TO USE RMB IN TRADE FINANCING, MORE REASON TO HOLD RMB FOR THE LONGER-TERM.

insights.claim: China's vice premier He Lifeng told the international audience that unilateralism is inadvisable and that China will improve its legal toolbox and add clauses to push back against unilateral sanctions, after Trump threatened more sanctions on banks clearing payments for Iranian oil.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low, Bloomberg China correspondent
insights.so_what: China hardening anti-sanctions tools raises the stakes for Chinese banks exposed to Iranian-oil payment clearing.
insights.quote: CHINA WILL TAKE THE NEXT STEP TO IMPROVE ITS ILLEGAL TOOLBOX ... AND COME UP WITH CLAUSES THAT ... PUSH AGAINST UNILATERAL SANCTIONS.

insights.claim: Chinese officials at the Lujiazui Forum announced plans to expand financial-sector opening, develop the onshore wealth management market, support Shanghai shipping insurance, strengthen the bond yield curve, and pilot new financial businesses including digital regulation in Shanghai.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Onshore wealth-management buildout could draw flows and compete with Hong Kong as a wealth hub, reducing incentives for illicit offshore capital flight.

insights.claim: The offshore renminbi reached its strongest level against the dollar in 40 months, around 6.75, framed as a bright spot for the market.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A 40-month RMB high supports the case that capital is finding China attractive despite weak equity benchmarks.

themes.theme: China equity underperformance and the earnings gap

insights.claim: From the 2009 bottom, the Hang Seng Index delivered roughly 250% total return over 18 years versus about 1400% for the S&P 500, with the gap attributed to the earnings story and the index's lack of large semiconductor and AI hyperscaler weighting.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The structural earnings/sector-mix gap, not just valuation, explains Hong Kong underperformance and frames any catch-up thesis.
insights.quote: TOTAL RETURN, 250 PERCENT OVER 18 YEARS ... 1400% ON TOTAL RETURN.

insights.claim: The offshore Hang Seng China index is pricing earnings growth around 10% to low teens while onshore China is pricing north of 20%, because the onshore market holds far more AI tech hardware, telecom, industrials and energy storage spanning the full AI supply chain.
insights.speaker: guest, equity strategist
insights.so_what: The onshore CSI 300 captures the AI supply chain and has performed like the Nikkei 225, while offshore lags; sector mix is the trade.
insights.quote: THE MARKET IS LOOKING FOR EARNINGS GROWTH OF NORTH OF 20% ... A LOT MORE AI TECH HARDWARE AND INCLUDING TELECOMMUNICATIONS, INDUSTRIALS, ENERGY STORAGE.

insights.claim: Hong Kong's HSCEI now trades around 10x forward earnings, below Taiwan and Japan, prompting some funds to trim major Asian tech names and buy offshore Chinese internet names in anticipation of eventual AI monetization and property/economy stabilization.
insights.speaker: Winnie Hsu, Bloomberg Asia equities reporter
insights.so_what: A ~10x valuation gap is the entry point for rotation into Chinese internet ahead of AI monetization.
insights.quote: HSC NOW ABOUT 10 TIMES FORWARD EARNINGS, LOWER THAN THE LIKES OF TAIWAN AND JAPAN.

insights.claim: J.P. Morgan raised its year-end emerging markets target by 27% on the Hormuz reopening, said the reopening is a buy signal for EM oil importers but expects them to underperform tech, and explicitly stated it embraces concentration in Korea and Taiwan targets.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: JPMorgan's 'embrace concentration' stance argues the scattered sector mix is exactly why mainland benchmarks underperform concentrated Korea/Taiwan.
insights.quote: WE EMBRACE CONCENTRATION

insights.claim: StashAway argues the Hang Seng lacks a catalyst because China's recovery is extremely slow with PMIs teetering around 50, and AI return-on-investment is obvious for hardware companies but not for the tech giants, which must show ROI in their numbers to spur interest versus Korea and Taiwan.
insights.speaker: guest, CIO of StashAway
insights.so_what: Hang Seng outperformance in H2 hinges on AI ROI showing up in tech-giant earnings, not just hardware.
insights.quote: FOR TECH GIANTS IT IS NOT SO OBVIOUS. WE NEED TO SEE THAT SHOW UP IN TECH NUMBERS.

themes.theme: DeepSeek price war and the China AI short call

insights.claim: A global tech research managing director is the only Street bear on the hottest Chinese AI stock, with a fair value around $407 derived from roughly 15x or 20x 2028 sales using Anthropic and OpenAI as valuation guides, versus the stock trading at 87x next-year while Anthropic's trailing valuation is about 21x.
insights.speaker: Felix, managing director of global tech research
insights.so_what: An 87x vs ~21x peer multiple plus an upcoming lockup expiry frames a high-conviction short on a crowded long.
insights.quote: ANTHROPIC TRAILING VALUATION IS RIGHT NOW 21 TIMES. TRADING AT 87 TIMES, THAT'S FOR NEXT YEAR. THAT'S HOW EXTREME IT HAS GONE UP.

insights.claim: Since DeepSeek released its V4 Pro model in April, token usage for the GLM model fell sequentially per extensive token-database work, indicating DeepSeek is taking material token share and pressuring rivals' usage.
insights.speaker: Felix, managing director of global tech research
insights.so_what: Falling token volume undercuts the bull case that depends on higher usage or higher prices for non-DeepSeek models.
insights.quote: THROUGH OUR EXTENSIVE WORK ON TOKEN DATABASES, THE TOKENS SEQUENTIALLY FELL SINCE THAT MOMENT.

insights.claim: DeepSeek left input/output token prices unchanged on its 5.2 model after raising them on 5.1, so running a task on the 5.2 model still costs about 5x more than DeepSeek's cheaper V4 Pro despite similar intelligence, establishing a cost floor that triggers a damaging LLM price war.
insights.speaker: Felix, managing director of global tech research
insights.so_what: If 'good enough' cheaper models win on price, frontier LLM pricing power collapses across Chinese AI names including MiniMax and Tencent.
insights.quote: IT STILL COST FIVE X MORE TO RUN A TASK WITH A 5.2 MODEL THEN IF YOU HAD DONE IT WITH DEEPSEEK V4 PRO, AND THE INTELLIGENCE LEVELS BETWEEN THESE TWO MODELS AREN'T THAT FAR OFF.

insights.claim: The bear argues the DeepSeek cost-floor thesis applies to essentially all other Chinese LLM models including MiniMax and Tencent, hurting everybody except DeepSeek, and that he is currently cautious on China internet as a whole and more optimistic on Western tech.
insights.speaker: Felix, managing director of global tech research
insights.so_what: Generalizes the price-war risk to the whole Chinese LLM complex, favoring Western tech over China internet.
insights.quote: I THINK IT HURTS EVERYBODY, EXCEPT DEEPSEEK.

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs cut its MiniMax price target by 14% to 860 from 1000 on concerns over lower pricing power and margins after the M3 launch, while J.P. Morgan upgraded the rival and named it the AI winner with the most pricing power.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Divergent sell-side calls on MiniMax versus its rival crystallize the pricing-power debate as the swing factor for Chinese AI stocks.

insights.claim: One analyst expects the AI bubble to potentially burst later this year and that a slowdown in the AI capex story would drive more traction into offshore HSCEI internet names, contingent on signs of reflation.
insights.speaker: Winnie Hsu, Bloomberg Asia equities reporter
insights.so_what: Frames an AI-capex bust as the rotation catalyst into cheaper offshore Chinese internet names.
insights.quote: ONE ANALYST IS EXPECTING THE AI BUBBLE TO BURST POTENTIALLY LATER THIS YEAR.

themes.theme: Anthropic export-control retreat and sovereign AI

insights.claim: A letter from US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warning Anthropic of criminal and civil penalties for failing to comply with export controls prompted Anthropic to disable its Mythos 5 models, restricting access not only for US adversaries but also for allies and Anthropic's own foreign-born staff.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A US lab unplugging its top model on government order is a reliability shock that allies and customers are now pricing into AI-supplier risk.
insights.quote: WARNING ABOUT ANTHROPIC OF CRIMINAL AND CIVIL PENALTIES IF IT FAILED TO COMPLY WITH EXPORT CONTROLS. THAT PROMPTED THE COMPANY TO DISABLE ITS MYTHOS 5 MODELS.

insights.claim: A Bloomberg Opinion columnist argues Anthropic's botched rollout became free advertising for Chinese open-source AI, which is monetizing the fear by positioning open-source models that can be downloaded and run locally so nobody can pull the plug on a whim.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Opinion columnist
insights.so_what: The episode is a tailwind for Chinese open-source model adoption and a reputational headwind for the US AI stack.
insights.quote: I REALLY THINK THEY COULD NOT HAVE FATHOMED BETTER FREE ADVERTISING FOR CHINESE OPEN-SOURCE MODELS.

insights.claim: The Anthropic retreat coincides with the start of a Silicon Valley price war as leaders question whether astronomical AI spending is worth it, ending the era of token-maxing, which amplifies the appeal of significantly cheaper Chinese AI models for automating routine tasks.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Opinion columnist
insights.so_what: Cost discipline plus a reliability scare accelerates enterprise migration toward cheaper Chinese models for routine workloads.
insights.quote: THE ERA OF TOKENMAXING IS COMING TO AN END AS LEADERS SEE THESE BILLS.

insights.claim: A French presidential candidate and Canada's prime minister cited the Anthropic disabling as a wake-up call to diversify away from foreign technology that can be unplugged at any time, fueling a durable sovereign-AI debate that bringing the models back overnight would not quell.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Opinion columnist
insights.so_what: Sovereign-AI diversification by allies is a structural new demand source for non-US and open-source AI providers.
insights.quote: EVEN IF THEY BRING FABLE AND MYTHOS BACK ON OVERNIGHT, I DON'T THINK THAT WILL BE ENOUGH TO QUELL THIS NEW DEBATE.

insights.claim: The bear suggested the US government may be restricting distillation, which could materially slow frontier model releases from both China and the US going forward.
insights.speaker: Felix, managing director of global tech research
insights.so_what: Slower frontier releases would reset the competitive cadence and the capex-justification debate across all AI labs.
insights.quote: MODEL RELEASES, FRONT YOUR MODEL RELEASES FROM BOTH CHINA AND THE U.S. WILL PROBABLY SLOW DOWN A LOT BECAUSE OF THESE CHANGES IN DISTILLATION.

themes.theme: AI positioning, rotation, IPO supply and gold

insights.claim: The Bank of America fund manager survey shows 56% still view the AI cycle as in a boom, only 21% see euphoria and only 9% think it is time to take profits, while a record 80% call chip stocks the most crowded trade and tech overweight was trimmed and global equity overweight cut from about 50% to 38%.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Record-crowded chip positioning with persistent FOMO is a fragility signal even as managers stay heavily long equities.
insights.quote: 80% SAY CHIP STOCKS ARE PROBABLY THE MOST CROWDED TRADE.

insights.claim: The market leadership has rotated away from AI tech into other sectors over the past two weeks, with US all-time highs led by industrials and financials, small caps outperforming the Nasdaq year-to-date, and the equal-weighted S&P beginning to outperform the cap-weighted S&P.
insights.speaker: guest, CIO of StashAway
insights.so_what: Broadening leadership argues for relative-value and equal-weight exposure over concentrated mega-cap tech.
insights.quote: THE THINGS MAKING ALL-TIME HIGHS ARE INDUSTRY OR FINANCIAL NAMES.

insights.claim: Eight of eleven US sectors had Q1 earnings growth forecasts above 10%, supporting StashAway's view that strong fundamentals extend well beyond the AI trade.
insights.speaker: guest, CIO of StashAway
insights.so_what: Broad earnings strength underpins a rotation thesis that does not depend on AI mega-caps continuing to lead.
insights.quote: 8 OUT OF 11 U.S. SECTORS HAD EARNINGS GROWTH FORECAST OF MORE THAN 10%.

insights.claim: Despite a heavy IPO year and SpaceX surpassing Amazon's market cap, StashAway expects net negative equity supply this year because IPO shares unlock gradually with the bigger free floats coming next year, against an estimated $1.9 trillion of global buybacks at historical pace.
insights.speaker: guest, CIO of StashAway
insights.so_what: Net negative equity supply is a technical tailwind this year, but next year's lockup unlocks are the risk to watch.
insights.quote: STILL THIS YEAR WE ARE EXPECTING TO SEE A NET NEGATIVE EQUITY SUPPLY. NEXT YEAR IT MAY BE, A BIT MORE PROBLEMATIC.

insights.claim: Only about 4% of SpaceX shares traded on its first day, so its market cap surpassing Amazon reflects thin float rather than true valuation, and lockup expiry after first earnings could add selling pressure and volatility.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Thin-float-driven valuations are a volatility trap; the post-earnings lockup expiry is the catalyst to watch.

insights.claim: StashAway calls gold a long-term buy even though it is the only asset class in negative territory year-to-date after being up 30% earlier in the year, citing ongoing fiscal policy, the 30-year US yield touching 5%, and rising Japanese rates.
insights.speaker: guest, CIO of StashAway
insights.so_what: A gold U-turn into negative YTD territory is framed as a long-term entry on the fiscal-debasement thesis.
insights.quote: GOLD HAS DONE A U TURN THIS YEAR. YEAR TO DATE I THINK IT IS THE ONLY ASSET CLASS IN NEGATIVE TERRITORY.

insights.claim: SpaceX formally agreed to take over AI coding startup Cursor in a $60 billion deal in which Cursor investors receive SpaceX stock at the implied equity value, advancing Elon Musk's effort to catch up on coding tools, while Goldman Sachs advised $1 trillion of M&A this year, the fastest any bank has reached that milestone.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Record M&A pace and a $60bn AI-tooling acquisition signal aggressive consolidation in the AI software layer.

insights.claim: Confidence in Indonesia, Southeast Asia's largest economy, is plummeting from its prior investor-darling status because of decision-making at the top, with a spontaneous president who dislikes long policy debates and a fractured advisory circle giving contradictory advice, pressuring the rupiah past the 80,000 mark.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Southeast Asia senior reporter
insights.so_what: Policy-credibility erosion at the top, not a single minister, is the structural driver of rupiah weakness investors must price.
insights.quote: CONFIDENCE IN SOUTH EAST ASIA'S LARGEST ECONOMY IS PLUMMETING.

insights.claim: Replacing Indonesia's finance minister would not be enough to placate markets because the business circle sees him as unable to explain Indonesia's fiscal and monetary positions, and what is needed is coordination across the president, ministers and central bank plus clear communication of policy multipliers toward the goal of pushing GDP growth past 8%.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Southeast Asia senior reporter
insights.so_what: A finance-minister swap is unlikely to be a durable rupiah catalyst; watch for genuine policy-coordination reform instead.
insights.quote: CHANGING THE FINANCE MINISTER IS SIMPLY NOT ENOUGH. IT IS JUST ONE PIECE IN THIS HUGE PUZZLE.

insights.claim: Bank Indonesia and the Philippines could deliver jumbo rate hikes this week as Asian central banks lead global tightening, with the RBA already more proactive than in previous rounds.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Asia-led tightening diverges from a Fed expected to hold, a setup for currency and rate-differential trades.
